Name: pac_bayes_layer_skipping_latency
Title: PAC-Bayes Layer Skipping for Latency-Critical Inference
Short Hypothesis: PAC-Bayes frameworks combined with spectral stability bounds can enable a mathematically grounded layer-skipping mechanism that achieves strict latency guarantees while maintaining model accuracy.
Related Work: Existing work on layer-skipping focuses on heuristic approaches or empirical validation, lacking theoretical grounding. PAC-Bayes frameworks have been applied to generalization bounds but not specifically to layer-skipping for latency-critical inference. Spectral stability bounds have been used in robustness analysis but not in the context of inference latency.
Abstract: Latency-critical applications demand strict inference time guarantees while maintaining model accuracy. This paper proposes a novel approach leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds to derive a mathematically grounded layer-skipping mechanism. By bounding the perturbation introduced by skipping layers, we ensure that inference latency is minimized without significant accuracy loss. We validate our approach on standard benchmarks, demonstrating that our method achieves strict latency guarantees while maintaining competitive accuracy. Our results show that the proposed mechanism outperforms heuristic layer-skipping methods, providing a theoretically sound and empirically effective solution for latency-critical inference.
Experiments: Implement the PAC-Bayes layer-skipping mechanism in a standard neural network architecture (e.g., ResNet).
Evaluate on standard benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) using latency and accuracy metrics.
Compare against heuristic layer-skipping methods and baseline models without layer-skipping.
Analyze the impact of spectral stability bounds on model robustness and inference time.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical bounds may be too conservative, limiting the practical benefits of layer-skipping.
The method may require additional computational overhead for spectral stability calculations.
The approach may not generalize well to architectures significantly different from the ones tested.
